title: Coppice hazel pea supports
subtitle: Twiggy hazel branches pushed into the ground as climbing supports
excerpt: A set of twiggy hazel pea supports made from fresh-cut coppice poles. Cut the branches, sharpen the bases with a billhook, push them into the ground. The traditional low-cost support that outperforms wire or mesh for tall climbing peas and beans.
sourceNotes: Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the hazel-branch pea support method. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics for coppice management and material selection. The method is consistent with descriptions in the Museum of English Rural Life open-access gardening and woodland archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven wood, still high in moisture content. Carves easily under hand tools.
A short length of round timber, here used to mean a coppice pole.

content.text: Pea supports made from twiggy hazel coppice poles are the oldest and simplest way to support tall-growing peas, and they work better than most alternatives. The branching structure of young hazel growth creates a natural web that climbing plants grip at every point. They are
, so they go into the ground easily and hold well. Cut them in late winter or early spring before the peas go in.

You need freshly cut hazel poles with side branches intact. The right size: a main stem about 20 mm in diameter and 150 to 180 cm long, with four to eight side branches of 8 to 15 mm diameter. Cut from two- or three-year-old coppice growth. One- or two-year growth is too thin; older growth is too heavy.
Birch whips and ash saplings make acceptable substitutes. Hazel is preferred because the branching pattern on a two-year hazel shoot is denser than on birch or ash of the same age.
Cut each pole to length with secateurs. For tall peas (1.2 m growing height), cut poles 150 cm long so 30 cm goes below ground. For dwarf peas, cut to 80 cm.
Use secateurs to sharpen the base of each pole to a rough point: four angled cuts converging to a point, each cut about 40 mm long. A sharp point drives into the ground more easily than a blunt base.

Push the poles into the ground in a row along each side of the pea planting, spacing them 30 cm apart. Push straight down, not at an angle. A slight mallet tap helps in hard ground. The side branches of adjacent poles should interleave: this makes the row self-supporting.

If the poles are in a double row for a wide bed, angle the top of each pole from the two rows toward the middle so they cross at 30 cm above the tallest expected growth. This creates a tent structure that is very stable in wind.

Pull the supports out in autumn before the ground freezes. Let them dry in a shed over winter. They can be reused the following year if the bases have not rotted. Two to three seasons is usual from a single cutting.
Wigwam support: tie five or six poles together at the top and splay the bases into a circle. Good for runner beans.
Hurdle panel as backdrop: use the same hazel poles in a wattle weave to make a low temporary fence for larger beds.

rows.symptom: Poles pull out of the ground in wind
rows.cause: Not pushed in deep enough, or tips not sharpened
rows.fix: 30 cm minimum in the ground. For sandy or very soft soil, push to 40 cm or tie adjacent poles together at mid-height.
